title: Känguru der Mathematik 2020 — Felix (Pré-Écolier, Grades 1-2)
source_organization: Känguru der Mathematik (Austria) / Kangourou sans Frontières
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper (kaenguru.at, via matematica.pt). For personal educational practice only.
answer_source: Official 2020_Answers.pdf answer table (Felix row), read and verified.

questions.question_text: The kangaroo goes up three steps each time the rabbit goes down two steps. When the kangaroo is on step 9, on which step will the rabbit be?
questions.solution: The kangaroo reaches step 9 after 3 jumps of 3 steps. In that same time the rabbit, starting at step 10, goes down 2 steps three times: 10 − 6 = step 4.
solution_steps: Kangaroo on step 9 = 3 jumps of +3 steps.
In the same time the rabbit moves down 2 steps three times = 6 steps down.
Rabbit: 10 − 6 = step 4.

questions.question_text: Julia has 5 pieces of plastic stacked on a table. What was the second piece she put on the table?
questions.solution: The piece put down second is covered by all the pieces placed after it but covers the very first piece. Following which shape lies under which, the second piece placed is the black circle (C).
solution_steps: The first piece is the one hidden under everything.
The second piece is covered by the later pieces but lies on top of the first.
That piece is the black circle, option C.

questions.question_text: Marco's father took a picture of his son standing in front of the car shown. Which of the drawings could represent this picture?
questions.solution: The boy stands in front of the car, so he should not be hidden by it, and the car must keep the same colours and direction as the reference. Only drawing E shows the boy correctly in front of the matching car.
solution_steps: The boy is in front, so the car must not cover him.
The car must match the reference (same direction and look).
Only drawing E fits both, so E.

questions.question_text: Every night the wizard Tilim forecasts the weather. A correct forecast earns 3 gold coins; a mistake costs a fine of 2 gold coins. After 5 days he neither won nor lost coins. How many times did he get the forecast right?
questions.solution: If he is right r times and wrong 5 − r times, then 3r (won) must equal 2(5 − r) (lost). 3r = 10 − 2r → 5r = 10 → r = 2. He was right 2 times.
solution_steps: Right r days earns 3r coins; wrong (5 − r) days costs 2(5 − r) coins.
Break even: 3r = 2(5 − r) → 5r = 10.
r = 2 correct forecasts.

questions.question_text: A magician takes animals out of his hat always in the same order. The pattern repeats every five animals. What will be the fourteenth animal he pulls out?
questions.solution: The order repeats every 5 animals. 14 = 2 × 5 + 4, so the 14th is the same as the 4th animal in the pattern, which is the frog.
solution_steps: The pattern length is 5 animals.
14 ÷ 5 = 2 remainder 4.
So the 14th is the 4th animal: the frog.

questions.question_text: Ana has the cards shown on the left. She chooses several of them to assemble the tower on the right. Which cards did she NOT use?
questions.solution: List the shapes that appear in the tower (blue circles, red triangles, green squares) and remove them from Ana's cards. The cards left over — two red triangles and a blue circle — match option C.
solution_steps: Find every shape used in the tower.
Cross those off Ana's set of cards.
The leftover cards match option C.

questions.question_text: Maria made a block using white cubes and coloured cubes in equal amounts. How many of the white cubes cannot be seen in the picture?
questions.solution: Since there are equal numbers of white and coloured cubes, work out the totals and subtract the white cubes you can already see. That leaves 2 white cubes hidden inside.
solution_steps: White and coloured cubes are equal in number.
Count the visible white cubes.
The hidden white cubes come to 2.

questions.question_text: Ana draws some shapes on a sheet. Her drawing has fewer squares than triangles. What could be her drawing?
questions.solution: For each sheet, count the squares and the triangles. Only drawing D has fewer squares than triangles.
solution_steps: Count squares and triangles in each drawing.
Keep only the one where squares < triangles.
That is drawing D.

questions.question_text: Which of the tiles below is NOT part of the wall next door?
questions.solution: Each corner tile in the wall shows the petals turning a certain way. Tile E has a petal arrangement that never appears in the wall, so it is not part of it.
solution_steps: Look at how the petals are arranged in the wall's tiles.
Compare each option to the tiles that actually appear.
Tile E does not match any, so E.

questions.question_text: A village of 12 houses has four straight streets and four circular streets. Each straight street has three houses and each circular street also has three houses. The map shows 11 houses. Where should the 12th house be placed?
questions.solution: Find the straight street and the circular street that currently have only two houses. The 12th house must sit where that street and circle cross — point D.
solution_steps: Each straight and each circular street needs exactly 3 houses.
Find the straight street and circle that are each missing one house.
Their crossing point is D.

questions.question_text: Five blocks are built with equal cubes glued face to face. In which of them was the smallest number of cubes used?
questions.solution: Count the cubes in each block, including the hidden ones. Block A is made from the fewest cubes.
solution_steps: Count every cube in each block, hidden ones included.
Compare the totals.
Block A uses the fewest cubes.

questions.question_text: Numbers were written on the petals of two flowers. One petal is hidden. The sum of the numbers on the back flower is twice the sum on the front flower. What is the number on the hidden petal?
questions.solution: The front flower's petals add to 1+3+5+7+9 = 25, so the back flower must add to 2 × 25 = 50. The visible back petals are 2+4+6+8 = 20, so the hidden petal is 50 − 20 = 30.
solution_steps: Front flower sum = 1+3+5+7+9 = 25.
Back flower sum must be twice that: 2 × 25 = 50.
Visible back petals = 2+4+6+8 = 20, so hidden = 50 − 20 = 30.

questions.question_text: Six figures were drawn, one on each side of a cube, shown in two positions. On the side opposite the kangaroo, what figure is drawn?
questions.solution: Using the two views, list which figures sit next to the kangaroo. The figure that never touches the kangaroo in either view is the green triangle, so it is on the opposite face.
solution_steps: From both cube views, note the figures next to the kangaroo.
The opposite face is the one figure never seen touching the kangaroo.
That is the green triangle.

questions.question_text: Maria writes whole numbers in the squares so that the sum of any three consecutive squares is always 10. She has already written 2 in the first square. What number should she write in the gray (fourth) square?
questions.solution: If every three consecutive squares add to 10, the numbers repeat every three squares. So the 4th square equals the 1st square, which is 2.
solution_steps: Any 3 squares in a row add to 10, so the pattern repeats every 3 squares.
Square 4 is the same as square 1.
Square 1 = 2, so the gray square = 2.

questions.question_text: Turning the card over on the top side, we see the kangaroo photo. Instead, turning the card over on the right side, what will appear?
questions.solution: A flip over the top edge turns the picture upside down; a flip over the right edge mirrors it left-to-right instead. Applying the left-right mirror to the kangaroo gives option D.
solution_steps: A top flip turns the image upside down (that gives the kangaroo).
A right-side flip mirrors the image left-to-right instead.
The mirrored kangaroo is option D.

questions.question_text: Grandma baked 23 cupcakes and wants to give the same number to each of her six grandchildren, eating what is left over. At least how many cupcakes will she have left to eat?
questions.solution: Divide 23 by 6: 6 × 3 = 18, with 23 − 18 = 5 left over. So she keeps 5 cupcakes.
solution_steps: Share equally among 6: 6 × 3 = 18 cupcakes given out.
Left over: 23 − 18 = 5.
Grandma eats 5 cupcakes.

questions.question_text: Tom has nine cards with three different shapes and three different amounts of drawings. He places them so each row and each column has the three shapes and the three amounts. Which card goes in the coloured box?
questions.solution: Treat it like a puzzle where every row and column must show each shape once and each amount once. The shape and amount missing from the coloured box's row and column point to card E.
solution_steps: Each row and column needs all 3 shapes and all 3 amounts, once each.
See which shape and which amount are missing in the coloured box's row and column.
The card with that shape and amount is E.

questions.question_text: Two equal trains, each with 31 numbered wagons, travel in opposite directions. When wagon 7 of one train is side by side with wagon 12 of the other, which wagon is side by side with wagon 11?
questions.solution: Because the trains move in opposite directions, the wagon numbers that line up always add to the same total: 7 + 12 = 19. For wagon 11, the matching wagon is 19 − 11 = 8.
solution_steps: Trains pass in opposite directions, so aligned wagon numbers add to a fixed total.
From 7 and 12: the total is 7 + 12 = 19.
Opposite wagon 11: 19 − 11 = 8.

questions.question_text: Tania bought 14 chocolates: 8 round and the rest square. Half were white and half were dark. Among the square chocolates, only two are not white. How many dark round chocolates did Tania buy?
questions.solution: There are 14 − 8 = 6 square chocolates, of which 2 are dark, so 4 squares are white. Half of 14 (= 7) are white in total, so the white round ones are 7 − 4 = 3. The round chocolates number 8, so the dark round ones are 8 − 3 = 5.
solution_steps: Square chocolates: 14 − 8 = 6; of these 2 are dark, so 4 are white.
White in total = 7, so white round = 7 − 4 = 3.
Round total = 8, so dark round = 8 − 3 = 5.

questions.question_text: Six different numbers from 1 to 9 are written on the faces of a cube, one per face. The sum of the numbers on each pair of opposite faces is always the same. Which number could be opposite the number 8?
questions.solution: The three visible faces are 4, 5 and 8. If opposite faces share a common sum S, then 8 + (opposite of 8) = S. Choosing 3 opposite 8 gives S = 11, which also works for 4+7 and 5+6 using only distinct numbers 1–9. So 3 can be opposite 8.
solution_steps: Opposite faces all add to the same sum S.
Try 3 opposite 8: S = 8 + 3 = 11.
Then 4+7 = 11 and 5+6 = 11 — all six numbers distinct, so 3 works.

questions.question_text: In a classroom each table has two chairs. Each boy sits with a girl at the same table, but four girls do not sit with a boy. There are 14 tables. How many girls are in the class?
questions.solution: The four girls with no boy fill 4 ÷ 2 = 2 girl-only tables. That leaves 14 − 2 = 12 tables, each with one boy and one girl, so 12 of those girls. In total: 12 + 4 = 16 girls.
solution_steps: 4 girls with no boy fill 2 tables (2 chairs each).
Remaining 14 − 2 = 12 tables each have 1 boy + 1 girl → 12 girls.
Total girls = 12 + 4 = 16.

questions.question_text: Rita numbered the circles from 1 to 8 so that the sum of the three numbers on each of the four sides of the square equals 13. What is the sum of the four numbers on the coloured (corner) circles?
questions.solution: The four sides add to 4 × 13 = 52, but each corner circle is counted on two sides. Since 1+2+...+8 = 36, the corners are the extra: 52 − 36 = 16.
solution_steps: Four sides total 4 × 13 = 52.
All eight numbers total 1+2+...+8 = 36; corners get counted twice.
Corner sum = 52 − 36 = 16.

questions.question_text: An arrow from one person to another means the first is shorter than the second (e.g. B is shorter than A). Which person is the tallest?
questions.solution: The tallest person is the one with arrows only pointing toward them and none leaving (nobody is taller). Following the arrows, that person is C.
solution_steps: The tallest has no arrow leaving them (nobody is taller).
Check each person for an outgoing arrow.
Only person C has none, so C is tallest.

questions.question_text: Maia the bee can only walk on coloured houses. How many ways can you colour exactly three white houses with the same colour so that Maia can walk from A to B?
questions.solution: Find every set of three white houses that, once coloured, completes a connected coloured path from A to B. Counting all such valid sets gives 16 ways.
solution_steps: A path from A to B needs the right white houses coloured to link up.
Count each choice of exactly three white houses that completes a connected path.
There are 16 such ways.
